Observation Details: One of a few unusually warm days in December - low 60s. Group of 3 hanging together up in a 70ft Shagbark Hickory tree. Stayed for a couple of hours. ID by sound - heard a lot of short notes, caught interest when heard brief robin-like song (don't usually hear robins this time of year). Short recording attached - short notes at end - unfortunately had muddy hands so not a good recording (edited out cars,loud neighbors,long bits when had recorder running and no sounds, other bird sounds) and couldn't grab a good picture (high up)-too grainy, out of focus
Robin-sized though more svelte, gray above, white below with white wing bars and wing patch, light iris likely a continuing bird.

Media notes: Many mimicked sounds! This Northern Mocking knew over 10 different species songs! It begins with a whole bunch of towhee sounds.   Sounds in order (including the mockingbird, excluding Common Yellowthroat, Song sparrow):  Eastern Towhee, Mockingbird , Red-Shouldered Hawk, Mockingbird, Blue Jay sounds, Northern Flicker sounds, N. Cardinal mixed?, Tree Swallow, ???, Great Crested Fly Catcher, tree frog?, Mockingbird, White-Breasted Nuthatch mixing into Northern Flicker, Blue Jay, ???, Carolina Wren, Red Shouldered Hawk, ???. Also others not on recording  Background: All Song sparrow song, All Common Yellow-throat song.
Observation Details: Watched singing complex song while perched on tree. So many mimicked sounds! List of mimicked sound included: Eastern Towhee, Blue jay, Northern Flicker, Carolina Wren, N.Cardinal, Tree Swallow, Red Shouldered Hawk, Great Crested Flycather, Eastern Blue Bird, (maybe Least Tern), White-Breasted Nuthatch and others! (Very impressive, at least 10 species sounds) Not just single songs from species but different call types from species.  Some sounds were very convincing if not for the wild mix.  I got a very interesting recording.

Recorder: iPhone Xs Max  using Merlin app. Edited with Audacity

Observation Details: I believe one of the birds singing in the recording is a NOMO. This assessment is confirmed by Mark Gawn and Dr. Dave Gammon, mockingbird song expert. In the foreground, one can hear YEWA, AMRE, HOWR, and very softly SOSP and NOFL. The NOMO sings in the background. The song is subdued, a bit like a fall song to me. Dr. Gammon indicates that these types of "acoustic traits are common in a mated male, especially when his wife is busy with incubation/brooding." The habitat is excellent, in brush near highway 2 across from a golf course. Unfortunately, I was not able to see the bird and the singing was brief. Please note that the recording was done by Merlin which did not recognize the NOMO due to the faintness of the singing (although it did identify a NOMO singing bout as NOCA!, see below).  In the recording, specifically, I pick out three different NOMO singing bouts, each consisting of 3-9 syllables, each repeated at least twice throughout. The first syllable is the descending trill first heard at 4s, then repeated 5 times until 15s. There is some subdued warbled singing, e.g. 25s, 28s, 56s, and at 1'12s which corresponds to the NOMO beginning to sing in typical "mockingbird" fashion. Follows a sequence of 9 cardinal-sounding syllables 1'13s, the return of the warble syllables 1'21s (in triplets, e.g. 1'27), a repeat of the "cardinal" syllables 1'34s, warbles 1'36s, initial trill syllables repeated 5 times starting at 1'37s, warble syllables 1'43s, and a last trill 1'46s.  Please note that this recording accompanies the "field impression" of the song, which was clearer, more continuous, and somewhat louder than conveyed by the recording made with an iPhone 11.

Media notes: The best mimid I've ever heard. The only species you can hear in this that are separate are the baltimore oriole and chipping sparrow. The killdeer, vireos, american robin, scarlet tanager, yellow-breasted chats, bg gnatcatchers, white breasted nuthatch, multiple woodpecker interaction calls, northern flicker, blue jays, eastern phoebe, and more are all mimicked by the mockingbird. He was right next to me and I have heard a lot of mimickry over time and if I was not looking at him, and they weren't all in the order one at a time, I would have been convinced these were all the species that he copied because he even managed to "throw" his voice somehow where sounds came from various directions. Really cool to watch him and listen.

Recordings are of the same bird.  Second, shorter recording is the more interesting one, for me, to listen to.  I finally got a good quality recording of this particular Mockingbird making a certain distinct sound that it makes often. There is a bit of wind noise,  distant traffic, and a Northern Cardinal in the background that otherwise would be excellent quality recording. Maybe an expert will listen and rate it. This Mockingbird spends most of its time in a bushy young tree (neighbor thinks is a crabapple, but I believe it's some type of Hawthorne) just out my backdoor, and pretty sure it's the same one for about three years now.

Observation Details: super cool encounter! one was around hallowell heard only as usual, and I knew that another one was around the barn, today first time close encounter with it.  was going to the labyrinthe area near the windmill on the east of fac when I started hearing mockingbird sounds, and then saw the bird perched conspicuously on the windmill that was not spinning there was minimal wind. it stayed there for at least 10 minutes in the entire duration I saw and recorded it. typical of mockingbirds to be up high and singing like that! as I stood under to observe the little sparrows, was amazed by its mimicry song and started recording it. during at least 5 minutes (see exact duration from audio), it put on quite a show. it did a VERY good eastern phoebe immediately, the "phoebe" sound including the buzz at the end, and then did some carolina wren songs also very good, then blue jay bell or tinkling sounds, then titmouse songs (peter peter peter), then a nuthatch sound, and then all other random vocalizations that it made up which was very impressive! hear audio recording although there are many species singing in the background too. anyways first time hearing a mockingbird song up close and being able to make out so many songs. last time i was under a short tree where mockingbird was singing but couldn't make out what it was mocking. after it finished its song it flew down and went east going go east side of horse range perching on bench near the goldfinch

Media notes: Adult and juvenile exchanging calls. Merlin app failed to identify calls as Mockingbird.
Observation Details: As I was passing Grebe Cove, a songbird dropped down into the cove and hid amongst the vegetation, less than 5 m from where I was standing. I saw it well enough to conclude that it was a juvenile Mockingbird. An adult Mockingbird was perched in a nearby Sycamore. The two birds started exchanging 1-syllable calls. The adult and juvenile calls sounded very much alike. To my surprise, the Merlin app failed to recognize the calls, even though they were loud and clear. My recording is attached. I never would have guessed Mockingbird had I not seen the birds.

Très grand répertoire de chants d’oiseaux qu’il imite dont quelques espèces qui ne sont pas présentes sur notre territoire comme le tyran huppé. Selon le propriétaire, l’oiseau serait depuis au moins 2 semaines.

Media notes: It was amazing hearing this mockingbird roll through so many other bird songs.  My Merlin app identified 9 other birds singing while this Mockingbird belted out the tunes.  Merlin thought that in addition to the Mockingbird there was a Common Babbler, Eastern Wood-Pewee, American Robin, Purple Martin, Orchard Oriole, Northern Cardinal, Eastern Kingbird, Northern Harrier, and American Kestrel.  After stopping the recording I also heard it imitate a Red-bellied Woodpecker, House Wren, and a Killdeer.  That bird was amazing!

Listened to concert. Came back to record and it was still perched and singing.  Sounds I recognized both before and during recording: eastern phoebe (3:50), three blue jay sounds including jays' own mimic of red-tailed hawks (3:21), killdeer (4:00), three Carolina wren songs and one call, Baltimore oriole song and chatter, red-bellied woodpecker call (4:52 and elsewhere), crickets (1:44), remote car lock beeps, reversing truck beeps, European starlings, white-breasted nuthatch song (:01, 5:21) and call, American robins including their alarm call (1:56 and elsewhere), gray catbird mew (3:08), northern cardinal, a mimic-response to a nearby singing tufted titmouse (4:23), northern flicker (4:58 and elsewhere), tree swallow, and house sparrow. Debatable scarlet tanager-informed chick-burr call at :53 and 4:49.  Kudos to Merlin for ID'ing nearly every sound as northern mockingbird. Though now that I look at the spectrograms, this bird's sounds are packed with upper frequencies. None of the birds it mimics would have equal volume from 1.5kHz to 20kHz. The power at 20kHz amazes me on its own, since that's ultrasonic sound that birds can't hear. Makes me wonder if that's how a mockingbird might distinguish itself to another.  Ended with a robin call as it flew a few feet over my head and off to another perch. The clipping at 5:57 gives a sense of how close it was.

It is as fascinating listening to this Northern Mockingbird mimic with great accuracy several bird species including Eastern Bluebird, Tufted Titmouse, Northern Flicker, AM Kestrel and Ring-billed Gull. Observation Details: That screaming alarm call was going on for at least thirteen minutes. I know when it stopped but may have been out of earshot when it started, so I can’t give an exact time.  At first, I was unable to get a visual on the Northern Mockingbird, as it was low, hidden deep in vegetation. I finally saw the Red-shouldered Hawk that the NOMO was warning everybody about, and then the Mockingbird showed up, close to the Hawk’s perch. Shortly thereafter, (a minute or two later) the RSHA left its perch and flew into a nearby tree, roughly fifty feet away. The NOMO followed close behind chasing the hawk whilst continuing to raise the alarm. As soon as the birds disappeared into the tree, the calling stopped.

Observation Details: Over by BlueCon side.

The vocal talents of Mockingbirds are insane. I've never closely observed one making so many different sounds. This bird had bluebird, peepers, song sparrow, cardinal, chickadee, killdeer, and probably more bird songs mixed in that I didn't recognize. It was absolute madness -- I will never trust my ears again. One of the coolest things I've seen in a while. Wish I could upload the video but ebird no longer allows that.

Continuing/returning bird well documented by Taylor Brown since 2021. Putting on a great concert today from the tree tops but very difficult to spot until it flew across the road and into a private backyard. Interesting to see meadowlarks and bluejays fly into the tree to investigate the calls!

Locality: St. Marks NWR--Mounds Trail & Tower Pond
Observation Details: Caught my attention with a Black Rail imitation… Bird had a huge repertoire and took over 5 mins of repeated singing ~2m and stopping for ~30s before repeating a call. Other species songs I could pick out were RTHA, RSHA, BAEA, NOHA, GRCA, BGGN, DEJU, BTGR, NOFL, RBWO, DOWO, FICR, LAGU/RBGU, ROTE, EAPH, KILL, ATFL, PUMA, TUTI, BHNU, the sound of a MODO’s wings, a hummingbird, and most impressively Common Nighthawk (both its calls and “song”) which likely hasn’t been here since November.

Observation Details: One that kept singing continuously as it flew back and forth between a tree and a pole. A song that fooled Merlin again and again.

One was on a utility pole by the bridge to river street, fairly far from where they usually hang out, loudly imitating a catbird, a hill, a Carolina wren, a phoebe (well enough that I thought I had an exciting new bird for my patch list), a red-bellied woodpecker, and a blue jay imitating a hawk. Lots of these were so good that if I hadn’t seen its beak opening or its throat moving in time with the calls, I would’ve been totally fooled. Listening back to the recording, I still can't believe it was all the same bird.

Ayant du sommeil à rattraper, je me suis levé un peu plus tard ce matin.  En ouvrant la porte pour écouter les oiseaux à l'extérieur, le premier chant que j'entends est celui d'un Cardinal rouge(une espèce très rare pour la MRC de Maria-Chapdelaine). Je me rends compte rapidement que c'est un moqueur qui fait ce chant. Je sors donc à l'extérieur pour aller confirmer un Moqueur polyglotte perché haut dans les grands peupliers, près du stationnement du personnel de l'école. Il est bien là(très audible), mais il est difficile à repérer visuellement au travers des feuilles qui commencent à peine à pousser. Il ne manquait que cette espèce de moqueur pour compléter le trio des espèces de moqueur observées à partir de ma cours. J'ai maintenant observé les 3 espèces de chez-moi.  J'ai fait plusieurs enregistrements, car j'ai été impressionné par le répertoire hyper-diversifié de ce moqueur. C'est le moqueur au chant le plus diversifié que j'ai pu entendre jusqu'à présent. Il s'agit fort probablement d'un individu qui provient du sud de la frontière ou du sud-ouest du Québec étant donné tous les chants qu'il a été en mesure de produire, et ce avec une exactitude déconcertante. Les quiscales, les merles et même les hirondelles se sont approchés croyant qu'un intru de leur espèce empiétait sur leur territoire. À ce moment, le moqueur changeait son chant, et les oiseaux territoriaux repartaient.  Pendant tout le temps de mon observation, il a imité le Martin-pêcheur, le Pic chevelu, le Pic mineur, la Crécerelle d'Amérique, le Geai bleu, le Moucherolle Phébi, le Tyran tritri, le Tyran huppé, l'Hirondelle bicolore, l'Étourneau sansonnet, le Moqueur chat, le Troglodyte de Caroline, la Mésange à tête noire, la Mésange bicolore, le Chardonneret jaune, l'Oriole de Baltimore, le Quiscale bronzé, le Cardinal rouge et le Tohi à flancs roux, soit au moins 19 espèces imitées... par un seul et même oiseau.  1ère fois que j'observe cette espèce de ma cours. Il s'agit de la 94e espèce notée à partir de ma cours, depuis que je m'y suis établi en septembre 2015 . C'est une 2e nouvelle espèce à vie qui s'ajoute pour ma cours en cette année 2022.  Toujours présent à 16h00 Photo et enregistrement audio à venir

Observation Details: I believe this bird has likely recently visited the Lower Rio Grande Valley or possibly even Mexico, based on the species it was mimicking: namely Great Kiskadee, Couch's Kingbird, and Green Jay. I have attached two audio files. One is the complete recording. Per eBird instructions, I broke the recording up slightly with 1 second gaps of silence to normalize the audio in different areas and to combine a second recording of the same bird. (I couldn't normalize everything at once because it got very windy near the end of the main recording.) I also attached a recording of the three LRGV species only, as a convenience for those that want to review it without having to listen to the longer recording in its entirety. This shorter recording is made from cropped portions of the main recording.  Below is a list of species and times in the long recording when they can be heard. I've also indicted some times where I'm unfamiliar or uncertain what the mockingbird is mimicking. Other times are simply what I would call 'standard mockingbird' where it's not apparent if another species is being mimicked or if the mockingbird is just making a lot of noise.  Species mockingbird is mimicking: 0 min, 12 sec - possibly Purple Martin. (Maybe, maybe not.) Same heard at 3 min, 35 secs. 0 min, 17 sec - possibly Black-bellied Whistling-Duck, albeit a little too fast and clipped. 0 min, 19 sec - Bell's Vireo 0 min, 21 sec - ??? (Strongly reminds me of something. Reminiscent of Gambel's Quail, albeit it faster than that species.) 0 min, 24 sec - Couch's Kingbird 0 min, 34 sec - Western Kingbird (or maybe Scissor-tailed Flycatcher) 0 min, 38 sec - ??? (maybe kingbird call) 0 min, 41 sec - ??? (possibly Great-tailed Grackle) 1 min, 00 sec - Ash-throated Flycatcher 1 min, 06 sec - Golden-fronted Woodpecker 1 min, 21 sec - ??? 1 min, 42 sec - Great Kiskadee 1 min, 45 sec - maybe a Great-tailed Grackle, Yellow-breasted Chat, or even an oriole. (Altamira Oriole is possible but probably too much of a stretch based on the recording.) 1 min, 48 sec - Bell's Vireo 1 min, 53 sec - Great Kiskadee 2 min, 08 sec - Verdin 2 min, 14 sec - Western Kingbird (starts slow with single call notes, rises to crescendo vocalization). This is complicated by the fact that there are a pair of Wesern Kingbirds present at times during the recording. But I believe a careful listen reveals this to be the mockingbird by the sloppy way he ends the kingbird and transitions into his next song. 2 min, 38 sec - Northern Cardinal / Pyrrhuloxia 3 min, 25 sec - Golden-fronted Woodpecker 3 min, 31 sec - Ash-throated Flycatcher 3 min, 35 sec - possibly Purple Martin. (Maybe, maybe not.) 3 min, 39 sec - Northern Cardinal / Pyrrhuloxia 3 min, 45 sec - Cactus Wren 4 min, 12 sec - Green Jay 5 min, 25 sec - Great Kiskadee (but also sounds vaguely similar to Squirrel Cuckoo) 5 min, 30 sec - beginning notes sound a bit more like Brown-crested Flycatcher but probably just Ash-throated Flycatcher 5 min, 45 sec - Scissor-tailed Flycatcher 5 min, 55 sec - Verdin 6 min, 54 sec - Bell's Vireo (probable but weak imitation)  Other species in the recording: 0 min, 04 sec - Green Heron (other times too) 0 min, 08 sec - Western Kingbird (other times too) 0 min, 11 sec - Killdeer (other times too) 0 min, 14 sec - Red-winged Blackbird (throughout the recording) 0 min, 39 sec - Eurasian Collared-Dove 0 min, 54 sec - Great-tailed Grackle (song). At first I thought this grackle (which also continues at 1 min, 13 sec) might be the mockingbird, but if you listen carefully, you can hear the grackle weezing while the mockingbird is mimicking Ash-throated Flycatcher. 1 min, 13 sec - Great-tailed Grackle (song) 3 min, 20 sec - Great-tailed Grackle (I think this is an actual grackle and not the mockingbird) 5 min, 02 sec - Orchard Oriole (call)

Observation Details: I believe this bird has likely recently visited the Lower Rio Grande Valley or possibly even Mexico, based on the species it was mimicking: namely Great Kiskadee, Couch's Kingbird, and Green Jay. I have attached two audio files. One is the complete recording. Per eBird instructions, I broke the recording up slightly with 1 second gaps of silence to normalize the audio in different areas and to combine a second recording of the same bird. (I couldn't normalize everything at once because it got very windy near the end of the main recording.) I also attached a recording of the three LRGV species only, as a convenience for those that want to review it without having to listen to the longer recording in its entirety. This shorter recording is made from cropped portions of the main recording.  Below is a list of species and times in the long recording when they can be heard. I've also indicted some times where I'm unfamiliar or uncertain what the mockingbird is mimicking. Other times are simply what I would call 'standard mockingbird' where it's not apparent if another species is being mimicked or if the mockingbird is just making a lot of noise.  Species mockingbird is mimicking: 0 min, 12 sec - possibly Purple Martin. (Maybe, maybe not.) Same heard at 3 min, 35 secs. 0 min, 17 sec - possibly Black-bellied Whistling-Duck, albeit a little too fast and clipped. 0 min, 19 sec - Bell's Vireo 0 min, 21 sec - ??? (Strongly reminds me of something. Reminiscent of Gambel's Quail, albeit it faster than that species.) 0 min, 24 sec - Couch's Kingbird 0 min, 34 sec - Western Kingbird (or maybe Scissor-tailed Flycatcher) 0 min, 38 sec - ??? (maybe kingbird call) 0 min, 41 sec - ??? Media notes: Northern Mockingbird belts out an eclectic, unpredictable mix of tunes, but in a characteristic fashion of uttering mostly 3-5 repeated phrases. Yellow Warblers and an Eastern Wood-Pewee sing in the distance. Background noise is from Laura-Marie taking items out of the trailer and setting up the bird banding station, as well as from wind and handling (a particularly loud pop at 0:21, unfortunately).  I captured this audio in .mp4 format by taking a video with my Samsung S5. Using the program Ocenaudio, I extracted the audio in .wav format and created a copy of the sound file. Then I applied minimal edits: only trimming the recording and normalizing the volume to -3 dB.
Observation Details: I heard this mockingbird singing just before 6 a.m. in a tree right next to where the bird banding table is located. It did not remain long. It flew north towards the entrance and chased a bird. I saw white flash from its wings. I have uploaded two still images and extracted audio from the video I took of it singing. I remember telling Jerry McWilliams that I found this bird next to the bird banding table, and he was quite surprised. He said that mockingbirds have been recorded at the entrance to Fry's Landing, but they are rarely found within that area.

A grayish bird, roughly robin-sized but more slender and with a longer tail, with white outer tail-feathers and white wing patches in flight. We watched it singing at the tip of a dying poplar, mimicking BLJA, MODO, AMRO, REVI, CARW, even RBWO. And we watched as it did its typical NOMO ‘dance’, flying mid-song straight up in the air a few metres and dropping again to its perch.

Media notes: On entend le moqueur polyglotte accompagné du chant d'un Bruant familier et des cris d'un Étourneau sansonnet.
Observation Details: Individu entendu depuis plusieurs semaine(possiblement plus d'un mois) et mentionné par Mélanie Barrette, une amie de Catherine Douesnard. Elle n'a réussi à l'observer physiquement et à le confirmer visuellement pour son identification que ce vendredi 11 juin. Lors de son observation l'oiseau se tenait aux alentours du salon de coiffure et du magasin Provigo du secteur Mistassini, tous les deux situés sur le boulevard St-Michel depuis jeudi dernier.   Mélanie et Catherine ont tenté de retrouver l'oiseau dans le même secteur, tôt samedi matin, le 12 Juin, mais sans succès. Catherine m'a aussitôt informé de la présence, très probable, de cet individu.  J'y suis donc allé ce dimanche en après-midi, et à vélo. J'ai arpenté toutes les rues à proximité du Provigo, tout en faisant de l'écoute, mais principalement pour déterminer où pourraient se trouver les meilleurs habitats potentiels pour ce moqueur. Après ma petite virée, le meilleur habitat m'a semblé être une bande d'arbustes et de feuillus près des terrains de tennis de l'école primaire Notre-Dame-des-Anges, tout juste derrière le motel St-Michel et l'entreprise Forestier LSTH Ltée, soit le long la piste d'entraînement pour la course. Ces 2 commerces sont accessibles par le boulevard St-Michel et situés derrière le Provigo.  J'ai donc fait jouer un seul coup de repasse à partir des terrains de tennis. Le moqueur est arrivé aussitôt, soit après seulement quelques secondes. Il se tient souvent dans les 4 gros tilleuls situés au coin sud-est du terrain de tennis, au milieu des équipement du terrain de jeux, mais aussi dans les feuillus, principalement un grand érable situé entre les équipements de terrain de jeu et le no civique, 22 rue Brisson. Le moqueur est venu se percher dans la lisière de feuillus derrière le motel, puis très actif, il a fouillé les feuilles et chassé les insectes pendant une bonne 15 aine de minutes sans être importuné de ma présence, se tenant parfois à seulement quelques pieds de moi. Au nombre élevé de chenilles que je l'ai vu capturer, c'est clair que la nourriture est très abondante et qu'il ne mourra pas de faim. Il devrait même être un peu plus bedonnant. Il n'a pas chanté à ce moment, mais le temps qu'il s'est promené et alimenté, il émettait un petit cri subtil, mais "buzzzzyyy" qui démontrait clairement son mécontentement de la supposé présence d'un autre individu de son espèce à proximité("call de l'oiseau").  Description physique: -Oiseau d'un gris pâle en continu sur le dessus de la tête, le cou et le dos.  -Tous les dessous: soit le ventre, la poitrine et les sous-caudales blancs. -Corps mince et effilé, -Oiseau de taille clairement plus petite qu'un merle ou qu'un étourneau(qui ont le corps plus arrondi),  -Longue queue noire évidente avec les rectrices externes blanches et sous-caudales blanches.  -Ailes noires avec de grandes plages blanches bien visibles de dessus en vol et encore plus visibles et évident vues de dessous. -2 minces barres alaires blanches qui contrastent avec le noir des ailes, -Iris de l'oeil jaune,  -Mince sourcil blanc. -Lore noir -Mince trait de moustache noir subtil, mais visible  Avant que je quitte, le moqueur est retourné en vol dans les tilleuls à la recherche de chenilles et d'insectes. Par la suite, il est allé se percher au sommet du lampadaire qui est du côté des tilleuls. De là, il a fait de nombreux chants typiques du Moqueur polyglotte, mais aussi plusieurs imitations qui démontrent toutes la vaste étendue de son répertoire sonore.   Il a imité parfaitement, coup sur coup, et ce en quelques secondes seulement, le chant du Pluvier kildir, du Geai bleu, du Merlebleu de l'est, de l'Étourneau sansonnet et il a terminé cette suite impressionnante de chants, par une imitation de Rainettes crucifères, des cris de grillons et de l'Hirondelle bicolore. Et pour finir, étant très territorial et tenace, il s'est mis à pourchasser, sans relâche, sur les terrains de tennis, un Étourneau sansonnet qui s'est approché trop près de lui. L'étourneau a dû aller se cacher, car il était vraiment entrain de passer, un sale quart d'heure.

Observation Details: Stopped in the rain, to check the weather on my phone, and I started to pay attention to this operatic singing that was ringing out from this Mockingbird about 6 feet away at eye level. I just turned and started to record as it continued, paying no attention to my umbrella and the racket the rain was creating bouncing off of it. I finally hit stop when it decided to fly off to perch on a fence as another Mockingbird entered into the area. I decided to not splice the audio and present it as I heard it (compressed it into mp3 format just so that it would be of a size acceptable for upload on ebird).

Observation Details: We were walking past the place where we heard and saw a NOMO in May 21, 2019 and April 28, 2020 and to our great surprise, we heard a bird with repeated multi-phrased songs. consisting of repeated series of the same note of greater than three times. This greyish-backed AMRO sized bird with dark wings and tail, was noted to be calling from the top of a cedar tree. The bird was whitish ventrally. The bird was seen clearly with binoculars from 50-70 meters away. The bird flew and the white flash of the distal dorsal wing was noted. No photographs of the NOMO were taken.  Audio was recorded.  The audio has sections of a very convincing imitation of an AMRO. There was an AMRO in the area but was not near the tree were the NOMO was singing from. Amazing vocal repertoire.

Observation Details: Nous découvrons le moqueur perché sur un fil électrique. Très actif ce soir, il se déplace constamment, tantôt sur un autre fil électrique, tantôt au sommet d’un poteau en bois d’Hydro-Québec puis, sur une clôture où bien perché sur une branche sèche d’un feuillu. Nous l’avons entendu vocaliser brièvement à quelques reprises. Vers 20h50, nous entendons le moqueur s’agiter et Christian qui est le plus près de l’événement constate que cet énervement est causé par la présence d’un chat qui provoque la sortie d’un deuxième Moqueur polyglotte qui joint l’autre pour le faire fuir de ce lieu.

Observation Details: Heard the bird first. Thought it was a melodious Rose breasted or a well versed Catbird/Brown thrasher.  I caught an initial glimpse of gray and white. I saw the bird fly with very aposematic plumage, (white, gray and black). The wings had distinctive centered white circular patches in flight.

Slim grey bird, dark eye line, dirty white breast, jumping up and turning cartwheels in air, showing bright white wing patches, fanning his long tail, and singing all the while he displays. Take ferry to Neebish, connecting onto the island’s 15 Mile Road headed eastbound. On the south side of 15 Mile, notice the mailbox at 9056 15 Mile that is labeled “Sanford Farm.” Pass this mailbox and look south where there is one-story red structure and wooden barn. The Mockingbird sits atop the barn and sings Killdeer, Great Crested Flycatcher, Hairy Woodpecker, Summer Tanager, and many more. He flies east and perches on the nearby farm equipment (where smoke was rising up today) or the fence posts.

Media notes: NOMO with a very large vocal display
Observation Details: We were walking on the road beside the graveyard when we heard a loud bird giving each phrase 3 or more times. We saw a robin sized, grey bird with  black wings and a "white flash"  in the dark wings noted when it  swooped  to the ground from a cedar tree in the graveyard. This bird called for the duration of our point count. This bird was differentiated from NOSH by the vocals, and the "large white wing (extending into the primaries) flash" in flight. It also had no black that we could see on the face/eye area. Bird with a  very large repertoire of phrases each repeated three or more times, especially the "cardinal, EAPH  and AMRO like imitations on the audio. NOMO was at this same site last year and I managed to get audio and photo's then. Sorry, no photo's this year just Audio..The size, shape and symmetry, the distribution of the black in the wings and tail (not on the face), and the audio seem to point us to the NOMO determination. We did have a NOSH at the same location in the recent past on the very top of a tree to the south, but the black mask was noted. The NOSH bird was silent and very shy the NOMO was very demonstrative vocally. We returned the next evening and the NOMO was still calling.but not seen. Today, April 30, 2020 we walked in that area, and did not hear or see the bird in passing. I hope this helps with the determination status.

Observation Details: Three recordings of the same bird. On all three he uses his signature phoebe-killdeer riff. The first is an early dawn song: quiet three-and-four-part clips of his usual repertoire. The second is a livelier but still somewhat reserved morning song. Neither is a full-throated broadcast. On the second tape you can pick out decent mimicry of grackle, cardinal, blue jay, crested flycatcher, wren and purple martin; in the passage at 0:37-0:40 he picks up on a nearby eastern phoebe. Third is night singing, with very quiet “keybob” vocalizations; at 1:40 he mimics a distant mockingbird, and at 1:58 does a brief phoebe-killdeer bit again.

Media notes: SONGS -  "Both male and female mockingbirds sing. They often mimic the sounds of birds (and frogs) around them, including shrikes, blackbirds, orioles, killdeer, jays, hawks, and many others. They go on learning new sounds throughout their lives. The song is a long series of phrases, with each phrase repeated 2-6 times before shifting to a new sound; the songs can go on for 20 seconds or more. Many of the phrases are whistled, but mockingbirds also make sharp rasps, scolds, and trills. Unmated males are the most insistent singers, carrying on late all day and late into the night. Brown Thrashers have a similar song, but the phrases are less varied and most are delivered just 2-3 times. Gray Catbirds can also sound similar, but their phrases are more nasal, hurried, and slurred." www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Northern_Mockingbird/sounds

Observation Details: Very late nesting record for this species.  I have seen fledged young in early August and have occasionally encountered nests with young in early August. Probably 6-8 days based on the few visible features on the feathers.  I only observed one bird in the nest, but vocalizations indicated possible presence of a second bird.

Media notes: Two mockingbirds scolding a Sharp-shinned Hawk with the birds giving both the typical aggressive 'rasp' (referred to as a 'hew' call in the BNA account) as well as a call (the pure, overslurred "Kew?" notes) not described in the BNA account. A review of the ML collection yeilds only one example (ML 23403) of this second call, and interestingly, it (along with the rasp calls) are being directed at an Accipiter (Cooper's Hawk)! Could this "Kew" call be Accipiter-specific? Another ML recording (ML197008) is of a NOMO chasing a Red-shouldered Hawk and only the rasp calls are heard. Other Behaviors: Mob. Equipment Notes: Used the Olympus LS-11 internal microphone.

Locality: Hébertville,boucle rg Lac Vert et riv. des aulnaies
Media notes: Moqueur polyglotte
Observation Details: Posé sur un câble électrique, un Moqueur polyglotte a été repéré sur le bord du la route lors de notre passage en véhicule. Nous constatons que l'oiseau chante sans arrêt tout en changeant fréquemment de perchoirs, se baladant de maison en maison. Les grandes séquences de chants du moqueur sont parfois entrecoupées de petites pauses où l'individu se jette au sol pour cueillir des insectes. Avec une acuité extraordinaire, l'oiseau imite à la perfection le chant de plusieurs espèces dont entre autres : Geai bleu, Pic flamboyant, Pic chevelu, Buse à épaulettes, Moucherolle phébi, Cardinal rouge, Merlebleu de l'Est, Merle d'Amérique, Sittelle à poitrine blanche, Hirondelle bicolore, Hirondelle noire, Carouge à épaulettes. Troglodyte de Caroline, grive sp., etc... Des photographies ont pu être prises. De plus, des enregistrements sonores de l'oiseau démontrent les grandes capacités vocales du moqueur. (CC).
